content.text: for LLM applications, built on top of LangChain. You wire typed components (inputs, models, prompts, retrievers, outputs) on a drag-and-drop canvas. The resulting flow is served as a REST API and can be tested interactively in the built-in Playground.

attrs.alt: Langflow canvas — Chat Input → Prompt Template → Language Model → Chat Output, with Data/RAG components and custom Python components

pip install langflow

# Start the server
langflow run
# Access at http://127.0.0.1:7860

# Specify host/port
langflow run --host 0.0.0.0 --port 7860

# Docker
docker run -p 7860:7860 langflowai/langflow:latest
Langflow runs a FastAPI server and serves the visual editor at
(or other provider keys) as environment variables before starting — Langflow reads them automatically.
a directed graph of components saved as JSON. Each flow gets a unique UUID used as the API endpoint.
a typed node with declared inputs and outputs. Components wrap LangChain classes (ChatOpenAI, PromptTemplate, Pinecone, etc.) or custom Python code.
each input and output on a component has a type (Message, Data, DataFrame, Embeddings, etc.). Langflow enforces type compatibility when you draw a connection.
a runtime override of a component parameter passed in the API request body — lets you customise model, temperature, or prompt without editing the flow.
the built-in chat interface accessible from the flow editor; useful for testing before exposing the REST endpoint.
and optionally override component settings in
The response nests the output deeply:
. Use a helper function to extract text in production code so you are not brittle to schema changes.
to the run endpoint to receive SSE (server-sent events). Each event carries a

import httpx, json

async def stream_flow(flow_id: str, question: str):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        async with client.stream(
            "POST",
            f"http://127.0.0.1:7860/api/v1/run/{flow_id}?stream=true",
            json={
                "input_value": question,
                "input_type": "chat",
                "output_type": "chat",
            },
            timeout=60,
        ) as resp:
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if line.startswith("data:"):
                    data = json.loads(line[5:].strip())
                    chunk = data.get("chunk", "")
                    if chunk:
                        print(chunk, end="", flush=True)

import asyncio
asyncio.run(stream_flow("your-flow-uuid", "Explain gradient descent step by step."))

Tweaks let callers override any component parameter without modifying the saved flow. Each key is the component's display name suffixed with its node ID (shown in the canvas by clicking the component).
Common tweaks: override the model name for cost routing, inject a per-user system prompt, set temperature to 0 for deterministic classification tasks.
Component node IDs are stable after saving a flow. Check them in the flow JSON export (
) turns any Python function into a draggable canvas node. Declare inputs and outputs using Langflow's typed

# Custom Python component — shows in the Langflow canvas as a draggable node
from langflow.custom import Component
from langflow.io import MessageTextInput, Output
from langflow.schema import Data

class TicketLookup(Component):
    display_name = "Ticket Lookup"
    description = "Fetch a support ticket by ID from the helpdesk API"
    icon = "ticket"

    inputs = [
        MessageTextInput(
            name="ticket_id",
            display_name="Ticket ID",
            info="The ticket identifier, e.g. TKT-001",
        )
    ]
    outputs = [
        Output(display_name="Ticket Data", name="ticket_data", method="lookup"),
    ]

    def lookup(self) -> Data:
        import httpx
        tid = self.ticket_id
        resp = httpx.get(
            f"https://api.helpdesk.example.com/tickets/{tid}",
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {self.HELPDESK_API_KEY}"},
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return Data(data=resp.json())
to a custom directory. Langflow hot-reloads custom components without a restart.
Python SDK — Run Flows from Code
Import and run a flow directly in Python without an HTTP call using
. Useful in CI pipelines, batch scripts, and unit tests.

# Run a flow programmatically using the Langflow Python client
from langflow.load import run_flow_from_json

result = run_flow_from_json(
    flow="path/to/exported-flow.json",
    input_value="What are the main clauses in this contract?",
    fallback_to_env_vars=True,   # read API keys from env if not in flow
    tweaks={
        "OpenAI-abc123": {"temperature": 0.1},
    },
)
print(result[0].outputs[0].results["message"].text)
, credentials stored in the flow JSON as environment variable references (
) are resolved from the current environment — no need to hardcode keys in the JSON.
in the request body to persist conversation history across turns. Langflow stores the memory keyed to this ID in its internal database.

# Session management via the API
import requests

SESSION_ID = "user-42-thread-7"

for message in ["Hello!", "What did I just say?"]:
    resp = requests.post(
        f"http://127.0.0.1:7860/api/v1/run/{FLOW_ID}",
        json={
            "input_value": message,
            "input_type": "chat",
            "output_type": "chat",
            "session_id": SESSION_ID,
        },
    )
    answer = resp.json()["outputs"][0]["outputs"][0]["results"]["message"]["text"]
    print(f"User: {message}")
    print(f"Bot:  {answer}")
    print()
Langflow flows are JSON files. Export them from the UI or via the REST API and commit them to git alongside application code.

# Export a flow to JSON for version control
# In Langflow UI: Menu (⋮) → Export
# Produces: flow-name.json

# Import a flow
# In Langflow UI: + New Flow → Import

# Programmatic export via REST API
import requests, json

resp = requests.get(
    f"http://127.0.0.1:7860/api/v1/flows/{FLOW_ID}",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer your-api-key"},
)
with open("my-flow.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(resp.json(), f, indent=2)
A CI pipeline can: export flow JSON on merge, diff against the previous version to detect breaking changes, and import the updated flow into staging/production via the REST API.
Comparison: Langflow vs Flowise vs n8n
the run API response nests the answer inside multiple layers. Extract it in a single helper function and unit-test against a real response to catch schema drift across Langflow versions.
Component ID vs display name in tweaks:
tweaks keys must match the node ID (
), not just the display name. Copy the exact ID from the canvas or the exported JSON.
Langflow caches the component registry at startup. After adding a new component file, either restart Langflow or trigger a hot-reload by touching the file.
using a short or non-unique session ID can cause two users to share the same conversation history. Use a UUID or
Langflow does not built-in support flow versioning or rollback. Keep flow JSON in git and automate imports to manage versions across environments.
Agentic Flows — Attaching Tools to the Agent Component
component implements a tool-calling loop. Attach built-in tools (Calculator, API Request, Python REPL) or custom components to the Agent's

# Agentic flow in Langflow — Agent component with tools
# Canvas wiring:
# Chat Input → Agent → Chat Output
#                 ↑
#         [Calculator, SearchAPI, CustomTool]

# Agent component settings (set in UI):
# - Agent Type: Tool Agent (uses tool-calling LLMs)
# - LLM: OpenAI gpt-4o-mini
# - System Prompt: "You are a helpful data analyst."
# - Max Iterations: 10

# The Agent node exposes a "tools" input port where you attach Tool nodes:
# - Calculator: built-in arithmetic
# - API Request: HTTP GET/POST to any endpoint
# - Python REPL: run Python snippets (sandboxed)
# - Custom Component: your TicketLookup from earlier

# At runtime, the agent decides which tools to call and in what order.

Each tool attached to the Agent port is registered in the LLM's tool schema automatically. The Agent decides the call order based on the user request and each tool's description — write clear, specific descriptions for reliable invocation.
to prevent runaway loops on ambiguous tasks. A value of 6–10 is a reasonable default; increase only if your workflow genuinely needs deep multi-step reasoning.
Decompose complex pipelines by having one flow classify or extract structured data, then pass the result to a second flow as a runtime tweak. This keeps each flow small and independently testable.

# Chaining flows — one flow calls another via HTTP
# Flow A (classifier) → returns category label
# Flow B (responder) — uses category label in its prompt

# In Flow B, add an Input component with type = "str" named "category"
# Then in your Python orchestrator:

import requests

FLOW_A = "classifier-flow-uuid"
FLOW_B = "responder-flow-uuid"
BASE_URL = "http://127.0.0.1:7860"

def classify(question: str) -> str:
    resp = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/v1/run/{FLOW_A}",
        json={"input_value": question, "input_type": "chat", "output_type": "chat"},
    )
    return resp.json()["outputs"][0]["outputs"][0]["results"]["message"]["text"].strip()

def respond(question: str, category: str) -> str:
    resp = requests.post(
        f"{BASE_URL}/api/v1/run/{FLOW_B}",
        json={
            "input_value": question,
            "input_type": "chat",
            "output_type": "chat",
            "tweaks": {
                "Prompt-xyz789": {"category": category},
            },
        },
    )
    return resp.json()["outputs"][0]["outputs"][0]["results"]["message"]["text"]

question = "My order hasn't arrived after two weeks."
category = classify(question)
answer = respond(question, category)
print(f"Category: {category}")
print(f"Answer:   {answer}")

This pattern is the Langflow equivalent of multi-agent orchestration: each flow is a specialist; your Python orchestrator is the router. It also makes it easy to swap individual specialists without rebuilding the whole pipeline.
in a test script to evaluate flow quality against a labelled dataset before merging changes to the flow JSON.

# Evaluate a flow against a test set
from langflow.load import run_flow_from_json

test_cases = [
    {"input": "What is 2+2?",                "expected": "4"},
    {"input": "What is the capital of France?", "expected": "Paris"},
]

for tc in test_cases:
    result = run_flow_from_json(
        flow="path/to/my-flow.json",
        input_value=tc["input"],
        fallback_to_env_vars=True,
    )
    actual = result[0].outputs[0].results["message"].text
    passed = tc["expected"].lower() in actual.lower()
    print(f"{'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL'}  input={tc['input']!r}  actual={actual!r}")
Run this evaluation in CI (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI) on every pull request that modifies a flow JSON file. Gate merges on a minimum pass rate to prevent quality regressions.
Langflow is a visual LangChain flow builder with typed component ports, a built-in Playground, and a REST API per flow.
for runtime overrides (model, temperature, system prompt).
and declare typed inputs/outputs — they appear as draggable nodes in the canvas.
runs a flow in-process from Python, bypassing HTTP — ideal for batch scripts and CI tests.
in the request body to persist multi-turn memory. Use deterministic, user-scoped IDs to prevent session collisions.
Export flows as JSON, commit to git, and import via the REST API to manage versions across environments.
